question_title: What will INDEC report as Argentina's April 2026 monthly inflation rate (IPC nacional)?
summary: Argentina's April 2026 monthly IPC is very well-constrained by multiple independent real-time consultora measurements. The key evidence: C&T measured 2.4% (GBA), EcoGo projected 2.5%, Equilibra estimated 2.4%, OJF 2.5-2.6%, LCG 2.6%, and Analytica at 2.8% as the high outlier. The BCRA REM survey median was 2.6% (though surveyed before March's upside surprise). Food/beverage deceleration (C&T: 1%, LCG: 1.7%) and education normalization (12%→5%) drove the decline from March's 3.4%, while transport (~4%) and clothing (~4%) prevented a sharper drop. My distribution centers around 2.55-2.60 with a slight rightward skew acknowledging INDEC's recent tendency to surprise above consultora consensus (March 2026: 3.4% vs REM 3.0%).

factors.description: Real-time consultora measurements (C&T 2.4%, EcoGo 2.5%) based on actual April price tracking - highest quality forward indicators
Broader consultora consensus spans 2.4-2.8%, with LCG at 2.6% and Analytica at 2.8% suggesting right tail
BCRA REM survey median 2.6% (46 participants, surveyed March 27-31, before March's actual 3.38% was known)
March INDEC surprised 0.4pp above REM consensus (3.4% vs 3.0%), signaling potential upside risk in INDEC vs consultora estimates
Food & beverage sharp deceleration confirmed (C&T: ~1%, LCG: 1.7%) and education seasonal spike ended (12%→5%)
Transport (~3.7-4%) and clothing (~4%) remain elevated in April, preventing sharper overall decline

reasoning:
**Forecast Summary: Argentina April 2026 Monthly IPC**

My distribution centers around **2.55-2.60%** with a median of **2.58%**, reflecting strong convergence across 6+ independent consultora estimates and the BCRA REM survey.

**Key findings:**
- **Real-time measurements**: C&T (2.4%), EcoGo (2.5%), Equilibra (2.4%) — these tracked actual April prices
- **Broader estimates**: OJF (2.5-2.6%), LCG (2.6%), Analytica (2.8%) — spanning a wider range
- **BCRA REM**: 2.6% median (but surveyed before March surprise was known)
- **Sector drivers**: Sharp food deceleration (~1-1.7%), education normalization (12%→5%), but transport (~4%) and clothing (~4%) remain elevated

**Distribution (80% CI):** 2.36% to 2.88%

The slight rightward skew accounts for INDEC's recent tendency to come in above private sector estimates (March: 3.4% vs 3.0% REM), and the fact that INDEC's national coverage may capture different dynamics than GBA-focused consultora measurements.

resolution_criteria:
This question resolves to the monthly percentage change ("variación mensual") of Argentina's national Consumer Price Index (IPC nacional) for April 2026, as first published by INDEC in its monthly IPC technical report. The figure can be checked at the INDEC IPC landing page (https://www.indec.gob.ar/indec/web/Nivel4-Tema-3-5-31) and in the corresponding "Informe técnico - Índice de precios al consumidor" PDF published on or around May 14, 2026.

For example, if INDEC reports the April 2026 monthly IPC nacional variation as 2.5%, this question resolves as 2.5. The value used will be the headline national monthly variation as initially released; subsequent revisions will be ignored.
fine_print: If INDEC delays the April 2026 IPC release beyond May 17, 2026 23:59 UTC, the question resolves based on the figure reported by [credible sources](https://www.metaculus.com/faq/#definitions) citing INDEC; if no such figure is available by that time, the question resolves as annulled.

factors.description: Real-time consultora measurements (C&T at 2.4%, EcoGo at 2.5%) based on actual April price tracking - these are the highest-quality estimates available
Broader consultora consensus (Equilibra 2.4%, OJF 2.5-2.6%, LCG 2.6%, Analytica 2.8%) spanning 2.4-2.8% range
BCRA REM survey median at 2.6% (but surveyed before March actual 3.38% was known, so somewhat stale)
March INDEC surprised 0.4pp above REM consensus (3.4% vs 3.0%), suggesting potential upside risk in INDEC vs consultora estimates
Food & beverage deceleration confirmed by multiple sources (C&T: 1%, LCG: 1.7%), education seasonal spike ended (12% → 5%), supporting lower April reading
Transport (~4%) and clothing (~4%) remain elevated in April, preventing a sharper decline

input.anchor: Consultora consensus center: 2.5% (weighted average of C&T 2.4%, EcoGo 2.5%, Equilibra 2.4%, LCG 2.6%, Analytica 2.8%)
input.assessment:
Argentina's April 2026 IPC is very well-constrained by multiple independent real-time measurements from consultoras. The key signal is that real-time price trackers (C&T at 2.4%, EcoGo at 2.5%) measured actual April price changes, putting the floor around 2.4%. The broader consensus spans 2.4-2.8%, with Analytica as the highest outlier. The BCRA REM survey at 2.6% was conducted before March's upside surprise, making it somewhat stale but still informative. The question resolves in just 10 days when INDEC publishes. 

The main risk is that INDEC's national figure may differ from the GBA-focused consultora measurements - March showed a 0.4pp upside surprise vs REM. However, that March surprise was driven by specific one-off shocks (education, fuel) that dissipated in April, so the consultora estimates should be more reliable this time. My distribution centers around 2.55-2.60 with a slight rightward skew to account for potential INDEC upside surprises.
input.calibration_notes: Distribution is well-grounded in 6+ independent consultora estimates and BCRA survey data. The SD of ~0.2 reflects both the spread of estimates and historical INDEC vs. consultora divergence. P10-P90 range of 2.35-2.88 captures nearly all plausible outcomes given the strong consensus. Right tail extends to ~3.2 at P99 to account for potential INDEC measurement surprises.
input.key_uncertainties: 1. How closely will INDEC's national figure match GBA-focused consultora measurements? 2. Whether transport/fuel impacts were larger nationally than in Buenos Aires. 3. Rounding effects at INDEC (to 1 decimal).
input.tool_audit: research() returned excellent data from both INDEC API, Infobae, and BCRA REM documents. web_search found multiple confirming Spanish-language sources. fetch_url extracted specific consultora estimates from Río Negro and El Destape. Polymarket data was available but at the event level only (single probability for the overall event, not broken down by buckets). Coherence links returned empty - no related Metaculus questions found.
input.process_reflection: This question type (near-term economic measurement with multiple independent estimates) is well-suited to the tools available. The research() sub-agent was highly effective at extracting INDEC API data and consultora estimates from Spanish-language financial media. The main friction was that Polymarket's API only returned the overall event probability rather than individual bucket prices, which would have been more informative. The scenario-mixture Monte Carlo approach works well for combining consultora spread with INDEC measurement uncertainty.

revision_history.reviewer_assessment:
## Verification Summary

**Hallucinated evidence:** None found. All six factors' core claims are well-grounded in the research trace:

- **Factor 1** (C&T 2.4%, EcoGo 2.5%): Confirmed by Infobae article (May 4, 2026) and iProfesional, both accessed and quoted directly in the trace.
- **Factor 2** (Equilibra 2.4%, OJF 2.5-2.6%, LCG 2.6%, Analytica 2.8%): Confirmed by Río Negro and El Destape fetch_url results in the trace, which list all four consultoras with matching figures.
- **Factor 3** (BCRA REM median 2.6%): Confirmed by BCRA REM PDF data extracted in trace, showing median 2.6%, Top 10 at 2.675%, surveyed March 27-31.
- **Factor 4** (March surprise 0.4pp: 3.4% vs 3.0%): Trace confirms "participants had estimated March at 3.0%" and actual was 3.38%, consistent with the stated ~0.4pp overshoot.
- **Factor 5** (C&T food 1%, LCG food 1.7%, education 12%→5%): All confirmed in Infobae research and iProfesional sources in trace.
- **Factor 6** (Transport ~4%, clothing ~4%): Transport confirmed at ~4% (Infobae/iProfesional) and 3.7% (El Destape); clothing at 4.0% (El Destape). Rounding to ~4% for both is a harmless approximation.

**Double-counting:** Factors 1 and 2 both draw on consultora estimates, and Factor 5 explains the sector-level drivers behind those estimates. For a numeric question where factors characterize evidence rather than serve as independent logit adjustments, this is not a significant concern.

**Wrong-direction factors:** No sign contradictions found. All factors consistently point toward the 2.4-2.8% range.

**Resolution criteria alignment:** The agent correctly targets the INDEC "variación mensual" national IPC figure, references the correct resolution source (INDEC IPC landing page and Informe técnico PDF), and notes the May 14 expected release date.

**Probability assessment:** The agent's tentative center of 2.55 is ~0.05 below their own simulation median (2.585) and ~0.05 below the simulation mean (2.604). This is a minor discrepancy that doesn't meaningfully affect the forecast. The stated range of 2.35-2.88 closely matches the simulation P10 (2.36) and P90 (2.88). Given the strong consultora consensus at 2.4-2.5% with broader estimates up to 2.8%, and a documented pattern of INDEC upside surprises, the distribution is reasonable. I find no evidence that the probability is meaningfully wrong.

**Counterargument quality:** The adversarial self-examination is substantive — it identifies the INDEC upside surprise pattern, the GBA-vs-national coverage gap, the Analytica outlier at 2.8%, and questions whether the P90 is too tight. This goes beyond token gesture.

**Confidence assessment:** 0.70 confidence is appropriate given strong real-time consultora data but residual uncertainty about INDEC divergence from private estimates.

**Tool audit accuracy:** The agent claims 0 tool failures, which matches the ground truth metrics (0 errors across 19 calls).

**Worldview consistency:** No conflicting worldview forecasts or research entries found. The two Argentina research entries in the worldview store are the same ones generated during this session's research phase.

No errors found warranting a warn or fail.
